metaTitle: Time Capsule Explained — Timelock Encryption with drand
metaDescription: Understand how timelock encryption works, what the drand network does, and when future-unlock encryption makes sense.

quick.what: Locks a message so it can only be opened after a chosen future time.
quick.use: Creating a reveal that becomes decryptable later without trusting one person to guard the key.
quick.notFor: Offline storage where you cannot depend on outside network availability.

intro.title: In plain English
intro.text: Time Capsule lets you encrypt now and unlock later. Instead of waiting for a person to reveal the key, the unlock depends on future public randomness that does not exist yet.

how.title: How it works
how.text: This tool uses the drand randomness network. Your message is locked to a future drand beacon round, and once that round arrives, the public beacon can be used to derive the decryption material.

useCases.title: Where you'd use it
items: Publishing a message or prediction that should become readable only later.
Building timed reveals for games, research, or public commitments.
Sharing a secret that should unlock at the same time for everyone.

mistake.title: Common mistake
mistake.text: People sometimes think drand is storing their secret. It is not. drand only provides future public randomness; your encrypted message still needs to be preserved separately.

history.title: History / fun fact
history.text: The strange part of timelock encryption is that the unlock value does not exist when you press encrypt. You are binding your message to a future event that the network has not produced yet.

security.title: Security note
security.text: Timelock encryption depends on the availability and integrity of the public randomness network. It is best for delayed release, not for situations where fully offline recovery is required.

sections.title: What drand actually is
sections.text: drand is a distributed randomness network run by multiple independent participants. Together they generate public randomness beacons at regular intervals.

sections.title: Why no single operator can unlock early
sections.text: The beacon is collectively generated. That means no one operator should be able to produce the future beacon value alone before the scheduled round arrives.

diagram.title: Encrypt now, unlock when the future beacon arrives
diagram.caption: Your message is tied to a future public randomness round, not to a password handoff by another person.
